overview: Syslog is the standard mechanism for network devices to generate, store, and forward event messages, ranked by severity, for troubleshooting and auditing.
definitions: Severity level: a 0-7 scale ranking how critical a logged event is, with 0 (Emergency) the most severe and 7 (Debugging) the least.
core_concepts: Messages can be sent to the console, stored in a limited RAM buffer, or forwarded to an external Syslog server over UDP 514.
NTP accuracy is what makes cross-device Syslog correlation meaningful.
protocol_operation: Device generates a message tagged with a severity level whenever a relevant event occurs.
Message is routed to whichever destinations are configured (console/buffer/server), filtered by the configured minimum severity.
packet_flow: Event occurs on device (e.g. interface down) -> message generated with severity level -> filtered against configured logging level -> sent to console / buffered in RAM / forwarded via UDP 514 to external Syslog server.

config_commands.purpose: Store log messages in a local RAM buffer of the given size.
Send log messages to an external Syslog server.
Set the minimum severity level of messages sent to the console.

verification_commands.purpose: Display the current logging configuration and buffered messages.

config_examples.title: Send warnings-and-above to a central Syslog server

troubleshooting.symptom: Syslog messages no longer reach the central server after its IP address changed.
troubleshooting.cause: The router's 'logging [host]' command still points at the old server IP.
troubleshooting.fix: Verify/update the logging host command to the new server address, then confirm with 'show logging'.

troubleshooting.symptom: Need only Warnings and above forwarded to the server.
troubleshooting.cause: Logging trap level was left at a lower (more verbose) severity threshold.
troubleshooting.fix: Raise the severity filter, e.g. 'logging trap warnings', so only level 4 and more severe messages are sent.

best_practices: Always pair Syslog with NTP so timestamps across multiple devices can be correlated during an incident.
security_notes: Retaining Syslog history is important for security auditing/forensics after an incident.
interview_questions: List the Syslog severity levels from most to least severe.
Why is NTP considered a prerequisite for useful Syslog analysis across a network?
exam_traps: Mixing up severity direction — 0 is the MOST severe (Emergency), 7 is the LEAST (Debugging).
enterprise_notes: Central Syslog collection (e.g. into a SIEM) is standard practice in enterprise NOC/SOC operations.
